content: The PostgreSQL query planner chooses a sequential scan over an index scan when the table is small or when a large fraction of rows match the predicate, because random I/O for index lookups costs more than streaming the heap.

To speed up slow Postgres queries, run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see the actual execution plan, add a btree index on the filtered column, and increase work_mem so sorts and hash joins stay in memory instead of spilling to disk.

Connection pooling with PgBouncer in transaction mode reduces the overhead of opening a new PostgreSQL backend per request, which matters under high concurrency where the per-connection memory footprint would otherwise exhaust the server.

Vector databases like Qdrant store high-dimensional embeddings and use approximate nearest neighbor search (HNSW graphs) to find similar vectors quickly, trading a small recall loss for a large speedup over brute-force cosine similarity.

Hybrid retrieval combines dense vector similarity with sparse keyword (BM25 / full-text) scoring, then fuses the two ranked lists so that exact term matches and semantic matches both contribute to the final ranking.

Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) re-ranks search results to balance relevance against diversity, penalizing a candidate that is too similar to already-selected results so the final set is not dominated by near-duplicate chunks.

Embedding models map text into a fixed-dimensional vector space where semantically similar sentences land close together; changing the embedding model or its output dimension requires re-indexing the entire corpus because old and new vectors are not comparable.

Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaling adds or removes pod replicas based on observed CPU or custom metrics, keeping latency stable under bursty traffic while releasing nodes back to the cluster when load subsides.

A blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments and switches the load balancer from the old (blue) to the new (green) release atomically, so a bad deploy can be rolled back instantly by flipping traffic back.

Circuit breakers protect a service from a failing downstream dependency: after a threshold of errors the breaker opens and fails fast for a cooldown window, then lets a single probe request through to test whether the dependency has recovered.

Rate limiting with a token-bucket algorithm allows short bursts up to the bucket capacity while enforcing a steady long-run request rate, which is gentler on legitimate clients than a hard fixed-window cap.

Prompt injection is an attack where untrusted content fed to an LLM contains instructions that hijack the model's behavior; defenses include separating trusted instructions from untrusted data and never granting tools blanket authority over scraped text.

Retrieval-augmented generation grounds an LLM's answer in retrieved documents so it cites real sources instead of hallucinating, but the quality of the answer is capped by the recall and precision of the retrieval step.

Context window management for long agent sessions uses a sliding window plus tool-result truncation and loop detection, so the conversation stays within the model's token limit without dropping the most recent and most relevant turns.

Sourdough bread rises from wild yeast and lactobacilli in a flour-and-water starter; the long fermentation develops sour flavor and breaks down gluten, and a hot dutch oven traps steam for a crisp crust.

Searing a steak in a screaming-hot cast iron pan triggers the Maillard reaction, browning the surface proteins for deep savory flavor, while resting the meat afterward lets the juices redistribute instead of spilling out.

Espresso extraction depends on grind size, dose, and pressure: too coarse a grind under-extracts into a sour shot, too fine over-extracts into bitterness, so baristas dial in the grinder until the shot pulls in about 25 to 30 seconds.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using light energy captured by chlorophyll in the chloroplasts, forming the base of nearly every food chain on Earth.
Tectonic plates float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere and grind past one another at fault lines; the sudden release of accumulated strain along a locked fault is what produces an earthquake.

Black holes form when a massive star collapses under its own gravity past the point where not even light can escape; the boundary of no return is the event horizon, and time appears to slow for an outside observer near it.
